assessment.question: My name ___ John.
___ you like coffee?
How ___ is your sister?
I have two ___.
She ___ TV when I called.
He ___ to Paris three times.
She has worked here ___ 2018.
There ___ a lot of people at the concert.
If I ___ you, I would study harder.
The book ___ by Hemingway in 1940.
She suggested ___ to the new exhibition.
By the time we arrived, they ___ all the food.
He made us ___ the whole exercise again.
Not only ___ to the meeting late, but he also forgot the report.
The results were ___ that the researchers decided to publish immediately.
The proposal, ___ had been discussed for months, was finally rejected.
He must ___ the keys in the car — they're nowhere else.
___ he known about the risk, he would never have agreed.
It is high time we ___ measures to tackle climate change.
She is widely regarded ___ the most important linguist of her generation.

grammar.question: She ___ to the gym three times a week.
grammar.explanation: 'She' is third-person singular, so we add -s to the verb: 'goes'.

grammar.question: I ___ that film last Tuesday.
grammar.explanation: 'Last Tuesday' is a finished point in the past, so we use Past Simple: 'saw'. Present Perfect is used without a specific past time.

grammar.question: If I ___ more time, I would learn Japanese.
grammar.explanation: Second conditional: 'If + past simple, would + infinitive'. Used for hypothetical or unlikely situations.

grammar.question: The report ___ by the manager yesterday.
grammar.explanation: Past passive = 'was/were + past participle'. The agent ('by the manager') tells us who performed the action.

grammar.question: You ___ see a doctor — that cough sounds serious.
grammar.explanation: 'Should' expresses advice or recommendation. 'Would' is for hypothetical situations, 'shall' for offers/suggestions, 'may' for possibility.

grammar.question: ___ Amazon is the longest river in South America.
grammar.explanation: We use 'the' with rivers, mountain ranges, oceans, and unique geographical features. It is always 'the Amazon', 'the Alps', 'the Pacific'.

grammar.hint: Use ___ with years, months and seasons
grammar.explanation: Use 'in' with years ('in 1995'), months ('in July') and seasons ('in summer'). Use 'on' with dates and days. Use 'at' with clock times.

grammar.hint: 'For' = period of time. '___ ' = specific point in time
grammar.explanation: Use 'since' with a specific point in time (2010, January, last year). Use 'for' with a duration (three years, a long time).
In reported speech, verbs shift back one tense. 'is watching' (present continuous) → 'was watching' (past continuous).

grammar.after: I met at the conference turned out to be a famous author.
grammar.hint: Use a relative pronoun for people
grammar.explanation: 'Who' (or 'that') introduces a relative clause referring to a person. 'Which' refers to things. In this clause 'who' is the object, so it can be omitted in informal speech.

grammar.question: If she ___ harder, she would have passed the exam.
grammar.explanation: Third conditional: 'If + past perfect, would have + past participle'. Used for imaginary situations in the past.
One-syllable adjectives ending in consonant-vowel-consonant double the final consonant and add -er: big → bigger, hot → hotter, thin → thinner.

readings.title: The History of Coffee
readings.text:
Coffee is one of the most popular drinks in the world. People drink more than two billion cups of coffee every day. But where did coffee come from?

The story of coffee begins in Ethiopia, in East Africa. According to legend, a goat herder named Kaldi noticed that his goats were very energetic after eating berries from a certain tree. Those berries were coffee beans.

From Ethiopia, coffee spread to Arabia, where people started to make a drink from the beans. By the 1400s, coffee was very popular in the Arabian Peninsula. People enjoyed it in special places called 'qahveh khaneh', which means 'coffee houses'. These were important social places where people talked, listened to music, and played chess.

In the 1600s, coffee arrived in Europe. At first, some people were worried about coffee and thought it was a dangerous drink. However, it quickly became popular, and coffee houses opened in many European cities. These became important meeting places for businessmen and writers.

Today, Brazil produces the most coffee in the world. Other important coffee-producing countries are Vietnam, Colombia, and Indonesia.

vocabulary.definition: liked or enjoyed by many people
an old story that may or may not be true
a person who takes care of a group of animals
having a lot of energy; very active
a piece of land almost completely surrounded by water
relating to meeting and spending time with other people
makes or grows something in large quantities

questions.question: How many cups of coffee do people drink every day?
Where did coffee originally come from?
When did coffee arrive in Europe?
Which country produces the most coffee today?

readings.title: Artificial Intelligence in Everyday Life
readings.text:
Artificial Intelligence, or AI, is becoming an increasingly important part of our daily lives. Although many people associate AI with science-fiction robots, the technology is actually all around us, quietly improving the way we work, communicate, and live.

One of the most common uses of AI is in our smartphones. Virtual assistants like Siri and Google Assistant use AI to understand and respond to our questions. These systems learn from our behaviour over time, becoming more accurate and personalised the more we use them.

AI is also transforming healthcare. Doctors can now use AI-powered software to analyse medical images and detect diseases such as cancer at earlier stages than ever before. This technology can identify patterns that might be invisible to the human eye, potentially saving millions of lives.

In the world of entertainment, AI algorithms decide what music, films, and videos to recommend to us. Netflix, Spotify, and YouTube all use sophisticated AI systems to analyse our habits, ensuring that we always have something new to discover.

However, AI also raises important questions. As machines become capable of performing more tasks, there are concerns about job displacement. Moreover, questions about privacy arise when AI systems collect and analyse vast amounts of personal data.

Despite these challenges, most experts agree that AI will continue to advance rapidly. Learning to work alongside AI will be one of the most important skills for the generations to come.

vocabulary.definition: more and more as time passes
to connect one thing with another in your mind
adapted or changed for a specific person
changing something completely
complex, advanced, and clever
being moved away from a usual place or job
very large in size or amount

questions.question: According to the text, where do we most commonly encounter AI?
How does AI help in healthcare?
options: It analyses medical images to detect diseases earlier

questions.question: What do Netflix, Spotify, and YouTube have in common according to the text?
options: They were all founded in the same year
They are owned by the same company
They all use AI to recommend content
They all offer the same type of content

options: AI is too expensive for most companies
AI may displace workers from their jobs
AI does not work in cold climates

questions.question: What does the text suggest about the future of AI?
options: AI will be banned in many countries
AI will continue to advance rapidly
AI will only be used in hospitals

readings.title: The Benefits of Learning a Second Language
readings.text:
In an increasingly interconnected world, the ability to speak more than one language has never been more valuable. While the practical benefits of bilingualism are well documented — better job prospects, easier travel, access to foreign media — the cognitive advantages are perhaps even more remarkable.

Research conducted over the past two decades has consistently shown that bilingual individuals develop what neuroscientists call 'cognitive reserve': a mental resilience that can delay the onset of dementia and other age-related cognitive decline by up to five years. The constant mental workout of managing two language systems appears to strengthen the brain's executive functions — the set of mental skills that includes working memory, flexible thinking, and self-control.

Beyond neurology, learning a second language fundamentally changes how we perceive the world. Languages do not simply translate reality; they shape it. The Japanese word 'komorebi', for instance, describes the interplay of light and leaves when sunlight filters through trees — a concept for which English has no single equivalent. Acquiring such vocabulary gives us new ways of noticing and articulating experiences we might otherwise overlook.

The social benefits are equally profound. Language is culture's primary vessel. When you learn French, you are not merely memorising vocabulary and grammar rules; you are gaining access to centuries of literature, philosophy, cuisine, and social customs. This cultural immersion fosters empathy and reduces the kind of misunderstanding that so often underlies international conflict.

Critics sometimes argue that English's status as a global lingua franca makes learning other languages unnecessary. However, this view overlooks the nuances of cross-cultural communication. Native speakers invariably communicate with subtlety, humour, and cultural reference that non-native speakers miss entirely.

Whether you choose Spanish, Mandarin, Arabic, or any other language, the investment pays dividends — cognitive, cultural, and professional — that last a lifetime.

vocabulary.definition: linked or related to one another
the ability to speak two languages fluently
relating to mental processes such as thinking and memory
the ability to recover from difficulties or adverse conditions
a brain disease causing progressive memory loss and cognitive decline
expressing thoughts or feelings clearly in words
a language used as a common means of communication between speakers of different native languages
always; without exception
benefits or returns gained from an investment of effort or money

questions.question: According to the text, what is 'cognitive reserve'?
options: A type of memory loss common in older adults
A mental resilience that can delay cognitive decline
A brain region responsible for language processing
A technique for memorising vocabulary more efficiently

questions.question: What does the Japanese word 'komorebi' describe?
options: A type of traditional Japanese food
The interplay of light and leaves when sunlight filters through trees

questions.question: How does the author describe language in relation to culture?
options: Language and culture are completely independent
Language is less important than cultural customs

questions.question: How does the author respond to the argument that English as a lingua franca makes other languages unnecessary?
options: The author agrees that English is sufficient
The author argues that true fluency goes beyond basic communication
The author suggests focusing on dead languages instead
The author avoids addressing this argument

listening.title: A Morning Routine
listening.instructions: Click Play to listen. Fill in the missing words as you listen.
listening.full_text: Every morning, I wake up at seven o'clock. First, I take a shower and brush my teeth. Then I get dressed and go downstairs to make breakfast. I usually have cereal with milk and a cup of coffee. After breakfast, I read the news on my phone for about ten minutes. Then I put on my coat and walk to the bus stop. The bus takes about twenty minutes to reach my office.
listening.display_text: Every morning, I wake up at [BLANK] o'clock. First, I take a [BLANK] and brush my teeth. Then I get dressed and go downstairs to make [BLANK]. I usually have cereal with milk and a cup of [BLANK]. After breakfast, I read the news on my phone for about ten minutes. Then I put on my coat and walk to the bus stop. The bus takes about [BLANK] minutes to reach my office.

listening.title: At the Doctor's
listening.instructions: Click Play to listen to the phone conversation. Fill in the missing words.
listening.full_text: Hello, this is Dr Martinez's office. How can I help you? Hi, I'd like to make an appointment for a check-up, please. Of course. Are you a new patient? No, I have been coming here for about three years. Could I ask your name? Certainly. It's Thompson, Sarah Thompson. And what's a convenient time for you? I'm free on Thursday afternoon or Friday morning. We have an opening on Thursday at half past two. Does that work for you? That's perfect. Thank you very much.

listening.title: Climate Change Facts
listening.instructions: Click Play to listen. Type exactly what you hear in the box below. You can listen as many times as you need.
listening.full_text: The global average temperature has risen by approximately one point one degrees Celsius since pre-industrial times. Scientists agree that this warming is primarily caused by human activities, particularly the burning of fossil fuels such as coal, oil, and natural gas. The consequences include rising sea levels, more frequent extreme weather events, and the disruption of ecosystems worldwide. To limit warming to one point five degrees, global carbon emissions must reach net zero by two thousand and fifty.
